data.title: Newsweek Reveals Fourth Annual ‘World’s Greatest Auto Disruptors’
data.description:
NEW YORK, April 15, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Newsweek, the modern global digital news organization, proudly unveiled today the honorees of its fourth annual World’s Greatest Auto Disruptors awards—a prestigious recognition of the automotive industry’s most forward-thinking visionaries, companies, and leaders. This year’s award ceremony will take place on April 15 from 4-7 p.m. at Newsweek’s headquarters at One World Trade Center, New York City.

These disruptors are redefining the future of mobility by leveraging artificial intelligence, electrification, sustainability, and advanced engineering to push the industry forward. From revolutionary powertrains to cutting-edge vehicle design, each honoree has played a pivotal role in shaping the next era of automotive innovation.

2025 World’s Greatest Auto Disruptors Honorees:

  • Visionary Disruptor of the Year – Michael Schiebe, Chairman of the Board of Management of Mercedes-AMG GmbH and Head of the Mercedes-Benz G-Class and Mercedes Maybach
  • Executive Disruptor of the Year – RJ Scaringe, Founder and CEO of Rivian
  • Design Disruptor of the Year – Volvo Cars
  • Research & Development Disruptor of the Year – Mercedes-Benz, Project Electric G-Class
  • Powertrain Disruptor of the Year – Porsche
  • Marketing Disruptor of the Year – Ford
  • Sustainability Disruptor of the Year – Kia
  • Community Disruptor of the Year – Bentley Motors
  • Technology Disruptor of the Year – Mercedes-Benz, Future Technologies Team
  • Legacy of Disruption Award – Tadge Juechter, Chevrolet

"The automotive industry is experiencing one of the most transformative periods in its history," said Dev Pragad, CEO of Newsweek. “These disruptors are not only responding to change but actively shaping what’s next. We’re honored to recognize their leadership and groundbreaking contributions."

"Being recognized as the Community Disruptor of the Year is an incredible honor for Bentley, and we sincerely thank Newsweek for this distinction at its New York event,” said Wayne Bruce, Chief Communications and DEI Officer. “At Bentley, we are dedicated to fostering an environment where everyone is empowered to do the best job possible as their authentic selves. Our commitment to inclusion and fostering a sense of belonging extends far beyond Crewe, as we believe that embracing diversity of people and thought not only fuels innovation but also drives global success.”

"For four years, we’ve celebrated the leaders and companies making the biggest impact on the automotive industry," said Eileen Falkenberg-Hull, Senior Editor, Autos at Newsweek. "Their innovation extends far beyond the vehicles themselves, influencing everything from consumer safety to environmental sustainability. These disruptors are setting the standard for the future of mobility."

The 2025 Auto Disruptors issue hits newsstands on April 18, 2025. This special edition includes two exclusive covers: one spotlighting RJ Scaringe alongside Rivian vehicles, and the other showcasing Michael Schiebe in Affalterbach. Both covers feature original photography captured exclusively for Newsweek.

For more details and the full article on the World’s Greatest Auto Disruptors Awards, visit: newsweek.com/auto-disruptors-2025

About Newsweek:

Newsweek is the global digital news organization built around the iconic 93-year-old American magazine. Newsweek reaches 100 million people monthly with its thought-provoking news, opinion, images, graphics, and video delivered across a dozen print and digital platforms. Headquartered in New York City, Newsweek also publishes international editions in EMEA and Asia.

data.title: Manufacturing Sales in Canada Revised Higher
data.description:
Manufacturing sales in Canada rose 0.2% month-over-month to CAD 72. billion in February 2025, better than initial expectations of a 0.2% fall, marking the fifth consecutive month of increases and the highest level of sales since September 2023.

The primary metal (+8.3%) and chemical product (+6.7%) subsectors drove the rise in sales while petroleum and coal product sales (-5.2%) posted the largest decline.

Manufacturing sales surged in four provinces, with Ontario and Quebec showing the largest increases and Alberta the largest decline.

Compared to February 2024, total sales rose 2.1%.

data.title: Wall St edges up as investors hold on to tariff relief hopes
data.description:
Key points:
  • Indexes up: Dow 0.18%, S&P 500 0.24%, Nasdaq 0.21%
  • Boeing falls after China halts deliveries
  • Bank of America, Citigroup gain after higher Q1 profit

Wall Street inched higher on Tuesday, as investors assessed the possibility of tariff relief for the auto sector, although signs of new levies on pharma and semiconductor imports kept the optimism in check.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday hinted at potential exemptions for the 25% tariffs imposed on imports of autos and auto parts.

But Federal Register filings showed the Trump administration was also proceeding with probes into imports of pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, as part of a bid to impose tariffs on the sectors.

At 09:36 a.m. the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 72.89 points, or 0.18%, to 40,597.68, the S&P 500 gained 13.16 points, or 0.24%, to 5,419.13 and the Nasdaq Composite gained 35.88 points, or 0.21%, to 16,867.36.

Financials gained 0.9% to lead sectoral gains, boosted by advances of 4% and 1.7% in Bank of America and Citigroup , respectively, after the banks reported higher quarterly profits.

"We believe "peak fear" is likely past us ... however, given volatility across stock and bond markets remains elevated, we remain defensively positioned as markets continue to be highly sensitive to incoming news flow on tariffs," said analysts at Wolfe Research.

Rapid changes in U.S. policy have sparked steep market selloffs, and left investors, companies and consumers confused over the outlook for policy and economic growth.

The main indexes had gained some ground on Monday after key electronics products were granted exemption from reciprocal tariffs.

Corporate results will be closely monitored over the next weeks for indications on how companies and consumers are coping with changes in trade policy.

"With all the uncertainty and all the moving parts that we have right now, it is not really in corporate management's best interest to do anything other than be cautious or provide no guidance at all, which is probably more likely," said Mark Hackett, chief market strategist at Nationwide.

Among other stocks, Boeing lost 1.3% after a report said China has ordered airlines in the country to not take any further deliveries of the company's jets.

Johnson & Johnson's shares reversed premarket declines to edge higher after the company beat Wall Street estimates for first-quarter revenue and profit.

Most analysts expect markets to remain volatile, until there's more clarity on tariffs. The S&P 500 has lost over 8% this year, and a BofA survey showed global investors have slashed holdings of U.S. stocks over the past two months.

The S&P 500's 50-day moving average (DMA) slipped below the 200-DMA on Monday, producing a "death cross" pattern that suggests a short-term correction could turn into a longer-term downtrend.

Richmond Fed President Thomas Barkin and Fed Board Governor Lisa Cook are scheduled to speak later in the day.

Advancing issues outnumbered decliners by a 1.61-to-1 ratio on the NYSE and by a 1.5-to-1 ratio on the Nasdaq.

The S&P 500 posted no new 52-week highs and no new lows, while the Nasdaq Composite recorded 10 new highs and 26 new lows.

data.title: OSI Systems Awarded $24 Million Contract for International Border Inspection Equipment
data.description:
OSI Systems has received an order from an unnamed international client valued at around $24 million for the company's security division to provide multiple units of its Z Portal drive-through inspection system for scanning large trucks, buses and cargo containers, the company said Tuesday.

The new inspection systems will be used for international border and security applications, with OSI also providing follow-on service and support as part of the contract award, the company said, without providing additional details.

Shares were edging 0.3% higher in early trading.

data.title: Soybeans Sink On Report Of China Halting Boeing PurchasesMarket Talk
data.description: Soybean futures are leading the grains complex lower, on news that China has directed airlines in the country not to place new orders for Boeing jets, according to Bloomberg News. For soybean futures, the sign of China ramping up the stakes in its trade war with the U.S. is a main pressure point. data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0955 ET - Soybean futures are leading the grains complex lower, on news that China has directed airlines in the country not to place new orders for Boeing jets, according to Bloomberg News. For soybean futures, the sign of China ramping up the stakes in its trade war with the U.S. is a main pressure point. "The U.S./China trade war appears to be intensifying again," says Doug Bergman of RCM Alternatives in a note. Soybeans fall 0.7%, while corn is up 0.1% and wheat sinks 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0948 ET - Gold futures tick higher on tariff uncertainty and short-term monetary policy outlook. Futures are up 0.2% at $3,233.60 a troy ounce, near Friday's record high of $3,263/oz. The exemption of some electronics products from so-called reciprocal U.S. tariffs provided some relief to the stock market, though this was quickly reversed by the threat the products could be subject to sectoral tariffs, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. The uncertainty both boosted gold's safe-haven demand and weakened the U.S. dollar as an alternative safe asset, further enhancing gold's appeal, Commerzbank says. The European Central Bank is generally expected to cut interest rates this week and it will likely consider further easing given U.S. tariff uncertainty, a boon for noninterest bearing bullion, analysts add. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0944 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper down 1.1% at $9,121 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum flat at $2,377 a ton. Indicators for Chinese copper demand are subdued, with March imports higher on-month but 1.4% lower on-year, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. First-quarter imports overall were down 5.2% on-year, the main reason likely being the spike in market prices on tariff concerns, Commerzbank says. Copper prices had risen a near six-month peak in mid-March of $10,046.50 a ton, before sharply sliding to as low as $8,105 a ton in the days following President Trump's so-called "Liberation Day" tariffs announcement. The levies were worse than the market expected, driving down expectations of global growth and therefore industrial metal demand. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0940 ET - Europe appears reluctant to strike back forcefully against President Trump's trade tariffs, Capital Economics' Andrew Kenningham writes in a note. EU members paused planned retaliation to U.S. tariffs after Trump set out a 90-day pause to blanket 20% duties on European goods last week, though steep U.S. levies remain on cars and some metals. Brussels' eagerness to pause countermeasures, plus its volubility over reaching a deal with Washington and the limits of its trade arsenal, suggests the bloc will maintain a "cautious and calculated approach," Kenningham says. Although the EU may have to retaliate to some extent if Trump persists, for now it will "threaten retaliation but...hope not to have to carry out its threats on any scale."(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0929 ET - Canadian inflation cools in March, and Ballinger Group foreign-exchange analyst Kyle Chapman says President Trump played a role. Inflation decelerates to 2.3%, and Chapman says the two biggest contributors to the downside--gasoline and airfares--can be linked to Trump's trade policy and the implications on global growth; and Canadian consumers opting to avoid travel to the US. Chapman, though, says the cooler inflation is not enough for the Bank of Canada to cut rates again on Wednesday. He says core inflation, which strips out volatile items like food and energy, "is too high to say with confidence," that the current trend is now disinflationary. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0916 ET - CIBC Capital Markets says the sharp deceleration in Canadian CPI in March reinforces its call for a rate cut Wednesday from the Bank of Canada. Inflation cooled to 2.3% year-over-year in March from the prior month's 2.6% reading. The BOC's preferred measures of underlying, or core, inflation remain close to 3%, or the top end of the central bank's 1%-to-3% target range, but CIBC notes the gauges eased on a month-over-month basis. BOC targets 2% inflation in setting rates. CIBC says that for BOC, the downside risk to growth from US trade policy should outweigh worries over inflation. Most economists expect BOC to hold its policy rate unchanged. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0847 ET - Higher-rated euro investment-grade bonds are likely to perform better than their lower-rated IG peers in the current environment of increased uncertainty, CreditSights analysts say in a note. Lower-rated investment-grade credit spreads are likely to widen to a greater extent as economic growth weakens due to the effects of tariffs, the analysts say. CreditSights prefers domestically focused single As and AA-rated euro corporate bonds over globally exposed BBB euro corporate bonds. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0832 ET - Volvo is highly exposed to the current volatile macroeconomic environment, but there are several factors that can support the shares in the coming year, RBC Capital Markets analyst Nick Housden writes. The company's truck business is more exposed to Europe than the U.S., which is a relative attraction as there is less uncertainty surrounding Europe, he says. In addition, the U.S. freight, and construction equipment markets, are already off peak-cycle levels, which should limit downside risk. Volvo also has a strong net cash position while shares more than adequately discount the risk of potential earnings downgrades, Housden says. RBC upgrades Volvo to outperform from sector perform and lowers its price target to 295 Swedish kronor from 307 kronor. Shares rise 2.3% to 254.30 kronor. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0754 ET - The Rubik's Cube-like supply chain for the auto industry would become almost impossible to manage if President Trump moves forward with tariffs on foreign-made auto parts, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. The president has floated the idea of delaying or pausing auto tariffs facing the industry, which is critical at the moment because more than 40% of the Big Three automakers' parts come from foreign sources, the analysts say. "Flipping upside down a global supply chain that has been in place for decades cannot happen overnight for the auto industry," they say. The analysts add that the focus should be on finished vehicles, as the concept of a U.S.-made vehicle with all domestically made parts is "a fairytale fictional narrative." (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0725 ET - The dollar and Treasurys look set to recover some ground in coming months after recent sharp falls in the aftermath of President Trump's so-called reciprocal tariffs, Capital Economics economist Jonas Goltermann says in a note. The tariffs seem to have resulted in a loss of confidence in the U.S. as a safe haven for its currency and bonds, he says. However, the U.S. economy will likely avoid an outright recession and the Federal Reserve will keep interest rates on hold this year, shifting rate differentials back in favor of the dollar. "We also think that bond market dislocations will ease." The DXY dollar index rises 0.1% to 99.7230. The 10-year Treasury yield rises 2 basis points to 4.384%, according to Tradeweb. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0657 ET - Supply of new euro high-yield credit is expected to decline in 2025 from 2024 levels due to increased uncertainty around U.S. tariffs, CreditSights analysts say in a note. CreditSights estimates euro high-yield new issuance for this year at 90 billion euro, down from its previous estimate of 110 billion euro. Net issuance of both euro investment-grade credit and high-yeild credit is expected to fall this year, with a large proportion of new supply going towards repaying maturing debt, the analysts say. "Acquisition-related issuance is likely to remain lighter-than-average." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0650 ET - Gold futures rise close to all-time records on strong safe-haven demand. Futures are up 0.4% at $3,238.40 a troy ounce, close to Friday's record high of $3,263/oz. The precious metal has also gained on reports of the Czech central bank further boosting holdings, alongside the Chinese central bank's higher gold import quotas for commercial banks. With central banks keeping up gold purchases, a continuation of the support seen throughout 2024, the precious metal's next leg higher is being supported by incremental purchases from the investment community, SP Angel analysts say in a note. ETF holdings have climbed again to 89.1 million ounces from 88.7 million ounces on Monday, though this remains well below highs in 2020 and 2022, SP Angel says. As such, ETF demand has room to grow further. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Bunge Global Is Maintained at Neutral by Citigroup
Trump Just Handed Credit Card Giants a Windfall -- Here's Why Investors Are Piling In
data.description:
15 April 2024 - The Trump administration just handed U.S. credit card companies a regulatory giftand investors are taking notice. On Monday, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau asked a Texas federal judge to formally toss out the $8 late fee cap rule, a move cheered by business groups that claimed the rule was overreach. That rule, a key piece of Biden's antijunk fee agenda, would've hit the bottom line of major issuers like Capital One and American Express . Now, with the rule on ice and likely gone for good, banks are breathing easierand so are their shareholders.

Judge Mark Pittman, a Trump appointee, had already blocked the rule back in December, calling it a violation of the Credit Card Accountability and Disclosure Act. That law allows reasonable penalty fees tied to costsnot a hard ceiling. Monday's joint filing from the CFPB and the business lobby confirms what many expected: Trump isn't defending Biden's fee clampdownhe's dismantling it. On top of that, a federal appeals court just cleared the path for trimming CFPB staff, another step in weakening the agency without triggering a full legal showdown.

Why does this matter for markets? Because fee income is big business. Late fees account for billions in recurring revenue, and with the cap gone, those earnings are safefor now. That regulatory clarity is bullish for credit card stocks, especially heading into earnings season. Trump's move just reopened a lane for higher margins. If you're holding COF, AXP, or any lender with over a million cardholders, you're likely sitting on a quiet win.

data.title: Blackstone Partners With Vanguard, Wellington Management to Offer Access to Private Investment Assets
data.description:
Blackstone is partnering with Wellington Management and Vanguard to develop vehicles allowing individuals to invest in both public and private assets that are now typically limited to institutional and accredited investors, the companies said on Tuesday.

The new collaboration, the first of its kind between the companies, will broaden access to multi-asset portfolios and draws on each of the firms' respective strengths, the partners said on Tuesday.

The three firms also will collaborate on developing simplified multi-asset vehicles combining both public and private markets as well as actively managed and index strategies, they said, adding more details will be disclosed in coming months.

Blackstone shares recently were 1% higher in recent trading Tuesday.

data.title: Bulgaria's jobless rate falls to 5.5% in March
data.description:
SOFIA (Bulgaria), April 15 (SeeNews) - Bulgaria's jobless rate slipped by 0.3 percentage points on the year to 5.5% in March, the Employment Agency said on Tuesday.

At the end of last month, there were 156,335 registered unemployed people in the country, the government agency said in a monthly report. This compares to 158,706 jobless people recorded at the end of February.

Some 16,779 jobless people started work with the help of labour offices in March against 11,868 the prior month.

There were 20,151 job vacancies on the primary labour market last month, up from 12,831 registered in February. The highest percentage of vacancies was reported in hospitality and catering with 23.4%, followed by manufacturing with 17.6%, trade and repair of cars and motorcycles with 13.9%, public administration with 8.6%, agriculture, forestry and fisheries with 5.8% and creation and dissemination of information and creative products, as well as telecommunications with 4.9%.

data.title: These Stocks Are Moving the Most Today: Boeing, Tesla, Bank of America, Citigroup, J&J, Netflix, Rocket Lab, and More
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel

Stock futures fell Tuesday with investors trying to make sense of mixed trade policy signals from the Trump administration.

These stocks were poised to make moves Tuesday:

Boeing was down 3.3% after a Bloomberg report said China ordered its airlines not to take any further deliveries of the company's jets as part of trade war between Washington and Beijing.

Tesla fell 0.6% to $250.75. The stock chart of the electric-vehicle maker on Monday formed a death cross, a trading term for when a shorter-term moving average crosses over a longer-term moving average. In this instance, the 50-day moving average moved below the 200-day moving average. A death cross often is a sign that a stock is losing momentum.

Netflix aims to achieve a $1 trillion market capitalization and double its revenue to $39 billion by 2030, according to The Wall Street Journal, which was told about the forecasts from people who attended the streaming company's annual business review meeting last month. Netflix also set a goal of tripling its operating income by 2030 from $10 billion last year, one of the people told the Journal. Netflix shares were rising 2% in premarket trading. The company is scheduled to report quarterly earnings on Thursday.

Bank of America posted first-quarter earnings of 90 cents a share, beating analysts' estimates of 82 cents a share. Revenue of $27.4 billion topped forecasts of $26.97 billion. Net interest income of $14.4 billion missed expectations of $14.9 billion. Shares of the second-largest U.S. bank by assets were rising 1.8%.

Citigroup posted first-quarter earnings of $1.96 a share, better than Wall Street estimates of $1.85. The stock rose 0.8%.

Healthcare giant Johnson & Johnson posted better-than-expected first-quarter earnings and revenue and boosted its full-year sales outlook to reflect the acquisition of drug developer Intra-Cellular Therapies. The company, which spans multiple sectors including pharmaceutical and biotechnology, reported growth across its innovative medicine and medtech segments during the quarter. The stock fell 0.9%.

Palantir Technologies rose 0.7% in premarket trading. The data-analytics company closed up 4.6% on Tuesday after the North Atlantic Treaty Organization said it would be using the company's artificial intelligence-enabled software to modernize its military capabilities. William Blair analyst Louie DiPalma said the agreement has "broader geopolitical significance than just another win for Palantir" amid Wall Street concerns that Europe wants to rely less on American defense contractors.

Shares of Apple were down 1% in the premarket session. The stock rose 2.2% on Monday following President Donald Trump's rollback of certain tariffs for the tech industry. Apple remains down almost 10% since the president's tariffs announcement on April 2.

Allegro MicroSystems was falling 9.6% to $19.89 after ON Semiconductor withdrew its proposal to acquire the chip maker for $35.10 a share. In a statement, ON Semiconductor said it determined there was "no actionable path forward." Allegro spurned ON Semiconductor's last proposal in early March that had an implied equity value of $6.9 billion. On Semi slipped 0.3%.

MP Materials, the largest rare-earths producer in the Western Hemisphere, was up 2.9% in premarket trading after soaring 22% on Monday. The stock jumped after Beijing halted exports of the metals, which are critical for electric vehicles, smartphones, and a host of other technologies.

Applied Digital, the digital infrastructure company, reported a fiscal third-quarter loss of 8 cents a share, narrower than analysts' estimates that called for a loss of 10 cents. Revenue jumped 22% to $52.9 million but missed expectations of $64.8 million. The stock fell 12%.

Dow Inc. fell 4.4% to $27.60 after shares of the chemical maker were double-downgraded to Underperform from Buy at BofA Securities and the price target was reduced to $28 from $44.

Earnings reports are expected later Tuesday from United Airlines, Interactive Brokers, and J.B. Hunt Transport Services.

Write to Joe Woelfel at joseph.woelfel@barrons.com

data.title: Tesla Stock Forms a 'Death Cross.' What That Means and What Happens Next.
data.description:
Al Root

Tesla stock was falling in early trading Tuesday as investors dealt with a new " death cross."

Shares of the electric vehicle maker were down 1% in premarket trading at $249.94, while S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were falling 0.1% and 0.3%, respectively.

The early move comes after Tesla's stock chart formed a new death cross on Monday. That's a trading term for when a shorter-term moving average crosses over a longer-term moving average. In this instance, the 50-day moving average is below the 200-day moving average.

The 50-day is about $289 a share. The 200-day is about $291 a share.

For traders, a death cross is a sign that a stock is losing momentum. It can be a bad omen.

Tesla stock, however, is always its own thing. It never does exactly what investors expect, like when it jumped about 90%, or $228 a share, in the 30 trading days after the Nov. 5 presidential election.

Tesla stock last formed a death cross in February 2024. It wasn't that bad. Shares were flat a month later and up 15% six months later. Shares didn't go up in s straight line, however. Tesla stock bottomed out in April 2024.

The 50-day moving average was below the 200-day until late July. Then a "golden cross" formed, a term for when the 50-day moving average crosses over the 200-day moving average. That's a good sign for traders.

Again, Tesla stock did its own thing. Shares were down about 8% from the golden cross formation into the Nov. 5 election.

It's tough to forecast what will happen with this death cross. Tesla is about to report first-quarter earnings, launch a new low-price model, and start a robotaxi service. Positive outcomes on any or all three events could drive Tesla stock higher.

The current death cross might reflect just how nervous investors are about Tesla sales, CEO Elon Musk's DOGE activities, and the impact of tariffs on the global auto industry.

Those are all reasonable fears for investors to track. Right now, the concerns weigh heavier than the potential positives, which is why Tesla stock, coming into Tuesday trading, was down 41% since President Donald Trump's inauguration on Jan. 20.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: Fiera Milano to Buy 51% Stake in Expotrans for Over EUR6 Million
data.description:
Fiera Milano (FM.MI) said Tuesday it entered a binding deal to purchase a 51% stake in logistics services company Expotrans for 6.3 million euros.

The transaction, which is expected to close in the second quarter, aims to extend the Italian trade fair and exhibition services company's value chain coverage, incorporate a core exhibition service into its business, as well as internalize logistics operations-related margins.

Fiera added that the deal includes a put/call option that allows it to buy the remaining 49% stake for up to 13.7 million euros, subject to the approval of its 2028 financial statements.
